data.title: The Trade Desk: As Cheap As Ever, Time To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Trade Desk, Inc. (NASDAQ:TTD) has been hit hard this year. After failing to hit guidance for the first time in many years, the company was met by the ongoing tariff dispute. The net result is that what was once an ultra-premium valued stock has now curiously found itself trading at more than reasonable valuations. I am not as confident as management that growth can re-accelerate meaningfully, but the strong balance sheet and cheap valuation more than offset such fears. Make no mistake, this is a solid buying opportunity for an important enabler of the digital advertising revolution. I am upgrading TTD to a buy rating.

TTD Stock Price

I last covered TTD in September 2023, when I downgraded the stock as I made the case that the stock was “priced for perfection.” At one point, the stock had risen over 50%, but as of recent prices, the stock is down 40% and has underperformed the broader market by around 60%. The stock has now been “dead money” over nearly the last 5 years.

The fundamentals always matter at some point, which in part explains the brutal drop, but also might foretell a future recovery.

TTD Stock Key Metrics

TTD is the largest independent demand-side platform (DSP), meaning that it helps advertisers to place ads across a variety of platforms. I like to think of TTD as being similar to “The Shopify of Advertising.”

TTD has historically viewed the growth of connected TV as being one of their more promising growth opportunities, but recently Amazon (AMZN) has emerged as a key competitor in this space. It is possible that competitive fears have contributed to the re-rating downward.

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 22% YoY revenue growth to $741 million, falling short of guidance for $756 million. This was the company’s first time in 8 years to miss guidance.

The company also missed guidance on the profitability front, generating $350 million in adjusted EBITDA versus the $363 million guidance.

The company ended the quarter with $1.9 billion of cash versus no debt.

Management guided for the first quarter to see at least $575 million in revenue (representing 17% growth) and $145 million in adjusted EBITDA. It is possible that the market did not like the company’s dip below a 20% growth rate profile.

On the conference call, management was adamant that their guidance miss was not due to competition of an overestimated market but rather due to “a series of small execution missteps.” Management showed confidence that they can accelerate top-line growth over time, noting that they still have only tapped 1% of the “$1 trillion TAM.”

Is TTD Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

After this year’s volatility, the stock traded hands at around 27x earnings. That represents a steep discount to the 84x 5-year average earnings multiple, but I caution that the stock had traded at ultrarich valuations heading into this year.

The stock traded hands at around 8x sales, with consensus estimates expecting no deceleration in top-line growth over the next decade. Consensus estimates look too optimistic, but I again note that the stock trades at a huge discount to the 24x 5-year average sales multiple.

I find it unlikely for TTD to return to its former valuation multiples, as I suspect that at least some of that valuation was earned from the company having been an indisputable “beat and raise” warrior, a title which it has since lost. The ongoing market turmoil might lead to declining growth rates in the near term. Even so, I find the stock quite attractive here at just over 8x sales. The company generated a 41% adjusted EBITDA margin in the last full year. I expect the company to sustain at least a 35% net margin over the long term. That places the current valuation at around 23x long-term earnings.

It bears repeating that TTD is still a rather small company, even after growing its revenues by over 10x over the last handful of years. I see the company continuing to grow rapidly as it helps consolidate the market outside of the tech titans. Even if we factor a deceleration in top-line growth into the 15% profile, a 1.5x to 2x price to earnings growth ratio (PEG ratio) would imply a valuation of between 22x and 30x earnings, or a stock price of $47 to $61 per share. The stock looks priced to deliver solid annual returns in the 15% range, even before accounting for any multiple expansions.

TTD Stock Risks

It is possible that TTD is being disrupted by competition. While the company has historically competed well against the likes of Alphabet (GOOGL), that does not imply that the company will be able to continue to do so in the future, let alone with the new AMZN threat. The stock might continue to be volatile in a market downturn, though the company’s strong balance sheet and GAAP profitability may offer some downside support. The stock is still not yet “dirt cheap” and it is unclear where top-line growth rates will bottom.

TTD Stock Conclusion

TTD has seen a dramatic fall from grace. The guidance miss and tariff turmoil have increased market uncertainty and risk premiums. The stock has fallen too much though and is looking attractively valued even if management is unable to re-accelerate top-line growth. The company’s strong balance sheet, GAAP profitability, and low valuation are all traits I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

data.title: Edgewell Personal Care Company: Buying The Plunge May Make Sense
data.description:
One company that has had a bit of a tough time recently is Edgewell Personal Care Company (NYSE:EPC). This is a firm that provides personal care products. Examples include razors, sun and skin care products, feminine care products, and more. This is a space that I have found interesting. And back in September of last year, I reaffirmed the company as a “Buy” candidate. This was in spite of the fact that the business had seen some weak spots leading up to that point. In particular, revenue had dipped slightly and some of its profit and cash flow figures had worsened year over year.

Because of how cheap shares were, I maintained that the company offered attractive upside for investors. But since then, shares of the company have fallen by 20.9%. This is nearly 10 times worse than the 2.8% decline experienced by the S&P 500 (SP500) over the same window of time. Some of this weakness can be attributed to a reduction in guidance provided by management when it comes to the 2025 fiscal year. But clearly, the big driver has been concerned regarding the broader economic circumstances we find ourselves in.

At this time, shares of the company remain cheap on an absolute basis. They are also cheap relative to other similar enterprises. Add to this the fact that management still expects financial performance to be slightly better this year than last year, and I believe that maintaining a “buy” rating is appropriate.

Still looking decent

The most recent fundamental data that we have regarding Edgewell Personal Care Company covers the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During this time, revenue for the business was $478.4 million. That actually represented a drop from the $488.9 million the business reported one year earlier. $4 million of this revenue decline can be attributed to foreign currency fluctuations. As an international business that generates a majority of its revenue from outside of the U.S., it's not surprising to see decent swings based on forex. However, the biggest hit, $6.5 million worth, came from an organic decline.

This organic weakness was not across the board. International revenue actually grew by 2% year-over-year, thanks to higher prices and increased volumes. The firm singled out its Wet Shave and Sun & Skin Care offerings as being particularly robust during that quarter. The real weakness for the company came from North America, with revenue dropping by 3.9% because of its Wet One’s and Grooming segments.

It would be helpful to look at the company on a segment-by-segment basis. For instance, the Wet Shave segment of the company saw revenue decline from $301.7 million in 2024 to $294.5 million in 2025. $3.2 million of the $7.2 million drop was attributable to foreign currency fluctuations. However, the firm did see an organic drop of $4 million because of a decline in volume associated with its Shave Preps and Women’s Systems throughout North America. North America experienced an organic volume decline of 6.6%.

The Sun & Skin Care segment performed much better, with revenue shooting up from $115.4 million to $120.6 million. This was driven by a $5.9 million increase in organic revenue, with the decline in the North America Sun Care category more than offset by strength in its Grooming and Wet One’s products. There is also the Feminine Care segment, which experienced some unfortunate weakness. Revenue plummeted from $71.8 million to $63.3 million. $8.4 million of the $8.5 million decline was organic in nature, with management blaming this on its Pads and Tampons product lines.

Management said that competition and promotional activity have proven problematic for it when it comes to its North America business. However, this does seem to be part of a worsening trend when it comes to the wellness industry in the U.S. Globally, this space is worth about $6.32 trillion. And by 2028, it's expected to climb to $9 trillion. But because of what one source described as "sticky inflation and an uncertain economy," consumer spending in this space has shown signs of weakness. That same source found that in three of the four wellness categories it looked at, average spending per consumer and purchase frequency had declined on a year-over-year basis in 2024.

With revenue falling, it shouldn't be surprising that the company's bottom line had worsened. The business went from a net profit of $4.8 million in the first quarter of 2024 to a loss of $2.1 million in the first quarter of 2025. Even if we adjusted for certain things, we would get a decline from $12 million to $3.3 million. Operating cash flow worsened from negative $72.9 million to negative $115.6 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a drop from $33.2 million to $29.4 million. Even EBITDA took a hit, contracting from $57.2 million to $45.9 million. It is worth mentioning that if we remove the hit associated with foreign currency, EBITDA in the first quarter of 2025 would have only been $57.1 million.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management does believe that organic revenue will rise by between 1% and 3%. Earnings per share are forecast to be between $2.54 and $2.74. This is down from the previous expected range of between $2.59 and $2.79. Management also said that adjusted earnings per share will be around $3.15 to $3.35. At the midpoint, adjusted earnings per share should translate to adjusted net profits of $158.3 million. Management had previously said that EBITDA would be between $356 million and $368 million. They now believe that it will come in around the lower end of that range. If we assume that adjusted operating cash flow will rise at the same rate that adjusted earnings will, we should anticipate a reading for it of $189.8 million for the year.

In the chart above, you can see what financial results were for 2024 as a whole relative to 2023. Obviously, the forecast for organic growth means that revenue should be higher than last year. However, the guidance for both adjusted earnings per share and EBITDA also imply a year-over-year improvement, if only marginally so. This is encouraging, but management has specified there is considerable uncertainty regarding the tariff situation. The most recent development was that there will be a hold of 90 days on most countries, during which time those nations will only have to deal with a 10% rate instead of the elevated rate that some had to contend with according to the prior decision by the current administration.

Even when the company announced financial results for the first quarter of its 2025 fiscal year on February 10th, management specified that the downward revision in guidance for earnings per share was driven in large part by a $29 million pretax charge that the company expects to incur this year as it continues to simplify its operations and improve both manufacturing and supply chain efficiencies. This includes organizational and operational changes in Mexico. However, the firm did also say that the outlook did not reflect potential impact from retaliatory tariffs or tariffs imposed by the U.S. Seeing as how the $29 million pretax charge is a new development, I would wager that it was influenced at least partially by the tariff situation.

In the chart above, we can see how shares are priced using historical results for 2024 and estimates for 2025. Shares are certainly trading on the cheap on an absolute basis. And in the table below, I stacked the company up against five similar firms. With each of the three valuation scenarios, I found that Edgewell Personal Care Company was cheaper than any of these other companies.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Edgewell Personal Care Company 9.4 7.8 7.7
Kenvue (KVUE) 41.1 23.7 19.7
The Estee Lauder Companies (EL) 132.5 11.2 39.4
e.l.f. Beauty (ELF) 33.0 93.7 17.5
Interparfums (IPAR) 21.3 18.6 10.9
Coty Inc. (COTY) 39.5 8.7 10.3

Takeaway

From all that I can see at the moment, Edgewell Personal Care Company strikes me as an interesting business that the market is discounting too much. I understand that there have been weak spots and the picture could get worse in the near term because of broader economic concerns.

But shares are cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to similar companies. Furthermore, even the reduced guidance provided by management currently indicates some improvement year over year. Add all of this together, and I do believe that maintaining a “buy” rating on the stock is appropriate.

data.title: Caterpillar: Not Bottomed Yet; Sales Down, Tariffs Up
data.description:
The heavy machinery industry forms the backbone of global infrastructure and development. The updated framework of buildings and land, energy and transportation needs, and new construction are all reliant on heavy machines and equipment.

Caterpillar Inc. (NYSE:CAT) (NEOE:CATR:CA) is arguably the most prominent heavy machinery company and is a leader in the industry across various metrics. The company manufactures and sells construction and mining equipment, as well as natural gas and diesel engines. Headquartered in Texas, the company was founded in 1925 and is in its centennial year. CAT mainly operates through three segments: Construction Industries, Resource Industries, and Energy & Transportation.

CAT is currently trading around $294, down from its recent peak of $406 in January. Historically, a very steady stock, this has been the steepest price drop the company has seen. Over the previous decade, CAT has gained a healthy 252%, including this recent fall off. The big question is whether to buy the dip, or will the free fall continue. I think, in the short term, the latter. I do believe CAT is a strong firm on many accounts, but with the current macroeconomic state, I rate the stock a sell. The impact tariffs will have on CAT internally, and the likely lower demand, are going to affect the company in the near term. Sales were down in 2024, and growth has dropped in the last two years. CAT has a strong presence in the industry, and I am not ruling them out for a future play; I just believe the bottom is not yet here for the stock.

Sales Down

CAT reported total revenue of $64.81 billion in 2024, down about 3% from $67.06 billion in 2023. The company makes small revenue on financial products, which increased, along with price realization. CAT has already noted they anticipate price realization to account for a 1% decrease in 2025 full-year sales.

Sales volume was $3.54 billion lower, led by a decline in their construction and resource industries segments, down 7% and 9%, respectively. Lower volume demand and unfavorable currency affected some regions. Geographically, CAT saw 3 of their 4 global regions decline in total sales, with the only positive being Latin America, gaining just 1%.

The only positive among sales is their energy and transportation segment, which was up 3% YoY.

Slowed Growth

Outside of 2020, from pandemic impacts, CAT had seen steady growth for many years in earnings, sales, and profitability. Since Q2 2023, financially the company has slowed and declined across several metrics. EPS is at 5.14, down from its 5.99 peak. Quarterly revenue is $1.1 billion lower than Q2, 2023. The latest earnings report also lowered Q1 2025 expectations for revenue.

Operating income was $3.88 billion in Q2 2023, and now was most recently $3.11 billion, 20% lower from 7 quarters prior. Gross profit has declined similarly, and the company has reported to anticipate lower operating profit margins in Q1 2025. Also being noted, was to anticipate less interest income and further currency headwinds in 2025.

Economic Outlook

We all know the impact the tariff landscape has had on the entire stock market. Unfortunately, the heavy machinery industry is going to be one of those areas that will be affected more adversely than others. CAT will get hit by tariffs on both the supply and demand sides as the company manufactures globally. While they attempt to keep product in the country it is manufactured, much of the product is shipped and is well engraved into the global supply chain.

Tariffs will have a negative effect on the firm's already declining global sales, as their products will become more expensive in the Chinese market, for example. Revenues will decline, and profit margins will be reduced as the added tariff cost cuts into profitability.

The 25% tariff on imported steel and aluminum will also impact their profitability. CAT uses these metals for various machines and will need to seek U.S.-based manufacturing even harder.

Demand Lowering

The economic uncertainty is clearly leaving many companies anxious and timid moving forward. CAT has warned of lowered equipment demand for the beginning of 2025. Heavy machinery is already costly for buyers and borrowers; tariffs and inflation are driving these expenses higher, leaving more and more Caterpillar equipment on hold.

Risks to Thesis

A bright side for CAT is the slight drop in cost of goods sold and total operating costs in 2024. But from a bear’s perspective, this, of course, came with lower sales and net income.

CAT does have a large manufacturing presence in the United States. This is what Trump wants for America, and if CAT can expand on its current “in-house” manufacturing, it could really give them an advantage over other companies fighting the tariff battle or expenses of moving operations to the U.S.

CAT is undoubtedly a leader in the industry and has enough financial strength to climb out of challenging positions. I want to make it clear that in the long term I am a believer in CAT as they have been a historically reliable stock and have a great global footprint in their industry. However, I think there are many other more attractive investment opportunities at the moment.

Competitors

Among peers, over the last decade, Deere & Company (DE) has outperformed CAT, although they serve a slightly different customer base. Cummins (CMI) and Komatsu (OTCPK:KMTUY) have lagged in stock gains. CAT has a P/E ratio of 13.4, a little lower than the sector average. Over the last 6 months, CAT stock has fallen off the most among the group.

Conclusion

CAT was up 8.42% over the second week in April as tariffs were halted and continued again. The decline in sales, and stagnant earnings and operating income aren’t necessarily a long-term concern, but especially given the 2025 guidance, there is not much to be excited about here. The firm will need to reassess their tariff approach as they will be negatively encapsulated in the ongoing global discussions. If they can manage to increase U.S. production significantly, they could be in a very good position. Right now, there are too many unfavorable factors and I suggest selling and reconsidering in the second half of 2025.

data.title: Viking Therapeutics: Trading The Volatility Of The Weight Loss Drug Market
data.description:
It has been roughly 3 months since my last Viking Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:VKTX) article, where I discussed how VKTX had dropped 50% despite strong clinical progress in obesity and NASH due to broader concerns in the GLP-1 market, largely triggered by Eli Lilly’s (LLY) Q4 revenue miss. However, I did point out that VK2735 and VK2809 were targeting multi-billion-dollar markets, so the ticker VKTX still had a high risk-reward profile with long-term upside potential. Since then, the ticker has dropped ~35% as the overall market reacts to the ongoing trade war.

In addition, Goldman Sachs (GS) recently initiated coverage on Viking with a Neutral rating due to a number of risks in the rapidly evolving obesity treatment market that could hinder VK2735’s prospects. However, Viking saw a surge in trading volume following Pfizer’s (PFE) announcement that they are dropping their oral weight loss drug, danuglipron, due to a subject experiencing liver injury in the trial. Although Pfizer will continue to develop other weight loss drugs, the benching of danuglipron takes one VK2735’s biggest competitors out of the game, and should improve the likelihood that Viking could be the first oral GLP-1 approved for obesity.

It looks as if the weight loss market remains incredibly volatile, with VKTX being one of the more popular tickers in the hunt to take some of the market from Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly. As a result, I am going to review my VKTX strategy to see if I need to adjust for the recent headlines.

I intend to review Pfizer’s decision to drop danuglipron and how that impacts Viking Therapeutics. Then, I will take a closer look at some of the concerns raised by Goldman Sachs. Finally, I take a look at VKTX’s charts to see if I need to adjust my strategy for these recent headlines.

Danuglipron Sidelined

Pfizer’s decision to discontinue development of danuglipron is a big event for the obesity treatment market. As an oral drug, danuglipron had an optimistic commercial outlook. However, a case of elevated liver enzymes in a subject in the once-daily study suggested the drug has the potential to cause drug-induced liver injury. Although the company reported that the subject was asymptomatic and recovered quickly, the event encouraged Pfizer to halt further development. I must point out that this was the second major setback for danuglipron. Previously, Pfizer had stopped their twice-daily version back in December 2023 due to tolerability issues. What is more, Pfizer jettisoned their other oral obesity candidate, lotiglipron, back in June 2023.

So, it looks as if Pfizer’s track record in the GLP-1 arena should be concerning for their investors, who were banking on one of the world’s best pharmaceutical companies to yield at least one operative drug in one of the fastest-growing therapeutic markets that is expected to exceed $150B by the early 2030s.

Impact on VK2735

Pfizer’s decision to move from danuglipron should open the door for smaller players like Viking to be considered a top contender for the next wave of obesity drugs. Viking has their own oral GLP-1/GIP dual agonist, VK2735. So far, the drug has posted some impressive Phase I and Phase II data, with some astonishing efficacy marks, along with a favorable safety profile. With Pfizer’s oral GLP-1 candidate out of the race, VK2735’s primary competitor won’t beat them to the FDA’s finish line and onto commercialization. So, VK2735 may stand to be the leading oral GLP-1/GIP dual agonist for obesity.

Keep in mind that VK2735 is a dual-agonist approach that mimics both GLP-1 and GIP pathways, similar to Eli Lilly’s injectable Zepbound, but in oral form. So, one could say that VK2735 could be a big disruptor if it gets on the market, and could quickly claim a respectable portion of that $150B market.

The Street is still expecting Viking to experience explosive growth as we head closer to the 2030s, and possibly hit blockbuster status with roughly $1.64B in 2030.

At that revenue, VKTX is currently trading at roughly 1.54x forward price-to-sales, which is under the industry’s average price-to-sales of 4x-5x. So, one could say that VKTX is trading at a discount for its projected revenue for 2030. Using those multiples, Viking would be valued around $58 to $73 per share. Considering the ticker is trading under $30 per share, we can say there is considerable upside in the next several years.

I must highlight that these projections were made prior to Pfizer’s announcement, so there’s a strong possibility that the Street will revise their estimates upward, considering that VK2735 is a viable candidate to fill the conjectural vacuum left by danuglipron’s withdrawal. To put this into perspective, danuglipron’s peak sales were projected to hit $2.4B by 2032. Considering VK2735’s promising clinical profile and oral formulation, I believe it has the potential to help capture at least a portion of that displaced demand.

Accepting the Challenges

Even with Pfizer’s failure, Viking Therapeutics still has a long list of strong competition from other small and mid-cap biotechs attempting to stake some claim to the obesity market in the coming years. However, even if Viking is the next one to make it through the FDA, they are entering the arena of one of the most competitive and resource-intensive drug markets in the United States, which is dominated by juggernauts Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly. Their semaglutide and tirzepatide GLP-1s already have proven efficacy in obesity, diabetes and have been shown to improve cardiovascular risk reduction.

So, even if Viking can get on the market, it is possible that Novo and Lilly just outmuscle them with their massive commercial infrastructure and nearly unlimited finances. It is possible that VK2735 makes it through the FDA and onto the market, but never achieves blockbuster status.

One of the concerns Goldman Sachs presented was the possibility that Viking will have to face high entry barriers to the market, including manufacturing, distribution, and commercialization. The bank estimates that VK2735’s Phase III trial will require $150M–250M, along with growing R&D costs, which should increase the company’s cash burn in the coming years. Although Viking has a healthy cash position of roughly $900M, it is possible the company will have to look for additional financing to fund their pre-commercialization efforts and get VK2735 onto the market. Investors need to consider that the company might consider dilutive financing to fund their next phase of development and transition to a commercial company.

The payer environment is also becoming a concern for GLP-1s. It is possible that payers impose tight access criteria for Wegovy and Zepbound due to the dramatic costs and large scale of patients that could require an obesity treatment. Although I am not the biggest fan of insurers, I have to agree that Wegovy and Zepbound should not cost 1.5-2.8 times as much as Ozempic and Mounjaro, which are the same drugs, but are just labeled for the treatment of diabetes. On the government side, the Biden administration was going to allow GLP-1s to be covered by Medicare and Medicaid, but the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services recently announced that they are nixing that initiative. Unfortunately, for obesity drug companies, that would have been roughly 7.4M potential patients. So, VK2735’s outlook could be dulled in the coming months and years as payers make moves to limit access and battle for lower costs.

One of the more concerning risks highlighted by Goldman Sachs was the growing number of innovative technologies that could be aimed at obesity. There is the possibility that we see “triple agonists” (GLP-1/GIP/glucagon), amylin analogs, leptin sensitizers, and other oral therapies that may quickly outperform VK2735 in the clinic and the market. Lilly’s retatrutide and Amgen’s (AMGN) AMG 133 were listed as potential competition that could produce better weight loss marks and could enter the market by 2026–2027. VK2735 is expected to hit the market not far ahead of them, so VK2735 may be outclassed in a year or two.

Now, I am not going to say that I share Goldman Sachs’ views with a $30 price target, but I have to concede that their points are valid. Yes, VK2735’s Phase II marks are impressive, and danuglipron’s withdrawal does improve its outlook. However, investors need to acknowledge there are considerable commercial, regulatory, and competitive headwinds that could be too much for Viking to handle. Therefore, I believe Viking cannot sit back and hope their Phase III data secures their success. I believe it would be wise for the company to strongly consider strategic partnerships or a reasonable buyout offer.

Don’t get me wrong, I am still a big fan of Viking and I believe in all of their pipeline programs. Moreover, I am maintaining my VKTX conviction level at 4 out of 5, which is the highest rating in my Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” portfolio.

Updating My Strategy

In my previous VKTX article, I discussed how the ticker was trading under the anchored VWAPS from October 2023, March 2024, and November 2024, indicating that all of the bullish momentum established in 2024 was lost. This was also reinforced by the bearish ratings on the Go-No-Go indicator, and the fact the share price is cutting through most uptrend support lines.

This was all set up by a bearish RSI cross with the Monthly RSI following a double-top event back in November. At that time, I was considering taking my position out of its “House Money” status as the share price dropped below my Buy Level 1 of $46.25 and was approaching my Buy Level 2 of $28.50, but I never decided to pull the trigger on another buy.

Since then, the ticker hasn’t posted a neutral or bullish rating on the Go-No-Go indicator. So, I have avoided pushing the buy button. Now, I am taking a harder look at the Daily Chart after the ticker fell below my Buy Level 2, and the share price bounced off the $20 support level. In addition, the RSI bounced off its uptrend line, suggesting we might have seen a near-term bottom, while also setting up a potential bullish divergence.

I am looking at the Anchored VWAP from the March 14th high as a key level in the near term. A break and hold above that indicator could suggest the bulls have established some momentum. The share price is trading just below that level ($25), so I will remain vigilant to see if I can find an opportunity to add in the coming days. Next, I will enter a comparable sell order around $50 per share, to return my position to a “House Money” status. Notably, this is down from my $80 sell level in my previous article. This adjustment is due to the chart's technical grades.

Long-term, I am still working “to amass a sizable core position for a long-term investment until the ticker graduates to my 'Bioreactor' growth portfolio.”

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chevron: Confirmed Leader In A Less-Bad Sector Nets Hold Rating
data.description:
At the beginning of April, Chevron Corporation (NYSE:CVX) was trading at $168. Today, the stock is trading at around $135. That's better than many stocks. But not enough to convince me that I should jump at it. In an environment where every day is a new adventure, and every night deserves an extra couple of peeks at the futures and foreign markets, the only energy stock in the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) is one I hope to own when this macro-mess is further along.

Ideally, at a much lower price, but with oil prices being a continued wildcard, I might ultimately convince myself it is range bound, long term. Which could be a gem of an opportunity, relatively speaking.

That current price is enough to CVX stock a forward price-to-earnings of 13.6x and a forward EV-to-EBITDA of 5.8x, but of course, there are structural risks associated with the thesis.

Risks for CVX

Fundamentally, Chevron is a great and very solid company. It generates a mountain of cash, is very profitable, and has a track record of allocation focused on cash generation and return of capital to shareholders via dividends and buybacks.

But it is still a company that can be interpreted as riskier, and this is mainly due to its dependence on oil prices (CO1:COM). In the chart below, you can see how revenue (and consequently other financial indicators and the return for CVX shareholders) follows Brent.

Consequently, with the macroeconomic volatility of the current scenario, CVX stock is trading at a certain discount. The materialization of tariffs, and a potential economic slowdown, has the potential to decrease demand for oil, and this greatly affects the price.

Not only is demand uncertain for the next few years, but so is supply. CVX itself is expected to increase production in the coming years, and other OPEC+ members will increase supply. Therefore, the result of a more nebulous demand with a growing supply is a lower oil price. Still, as the scenario is volatile, we could see a pick-up in the coming quarters in both demand and possible production cuts by OPEC, resulting in a recovery in the price of Brent.

In addition to this, other risks for Chevron are also important to monitor, such as regulation and taxes in this type of operation, mainly related to carbon emissions, taxes on fossil fuels and other trends related to the energy transition. For the long term, one of the main points to monitor is the discipline of capital allocation, i.e. whether the CapEx realized is actually bringing a return for the company while CVX adapts to the new environments.

The Clearest Part for CVX Stockholders

But there are still positive parts to the thesis, even with this uncertainty hanging in the air. By 2026, the company expects $10 billion of additional free cash flow due mainly to new fields such as TCO (whose first oil has already occurred) and Permian, but also through efficiency improvements that have the effect of reducing costs.

In other words, there is a dependence on the price of Brent, but at the same time, the company can mitigate this by being more efficient in its cost structure (lifting cost) and expenses, as well as continuing to increase production.

With this expected increase in Free Cash Flow, even with Brent at relatively low levels (such as $60, which would still be an increase of $9 billion, and even with the recent drop it is still at $65), Chevron should continue to generate a very interesting amount of free cash flow that will allow it to remunerate its shareholders, as shown in the chart below.

The dividend safety also receives a B score, mainly due to the strong balance sheet CVX has.

Is there a risk of Brent going to $40 in the short or medium term? There is, in the case of recessions, but even so, for a diversified portfolio, exposure to the oil industry through a solid company (with hedges, options) allows you not only to collect returns on Brent's eventual recovery, but also to collect interesting dividends over time.

CVX's Old-Fashioned Chart

The dividends are excluded in this chart, but the price-only view shows a fairly tight range for some time now. This makes CVX appear more like a throwback to decades ago, when energy and utility stocks looked like this a lot. Banks too, before they entered so many other businesses.

As my regular readers and investing group subscribers know well, I'm in no great mood to buy stocks unhedged. And this one does not have enough upside potential, nor enough volatility, to approach it via that route yet. So I'll bide my time and express my current take with a hold rating.

data.title: Taysha Gene Therapies: Sell Rating On Uncertain Pipeline Prospects And Limited Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

We initiate coverage on Taysha Gene Therapies, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSHA) with a Sell rating. In our view, the company’s valuation fails to compensate for the high binary clinical risk of its lead Rett syndrome program and the potentially narrow market opportunity. While Taysha holds a solid cash position, we think limited efficacy data, a potential capital raise in 2026, and competitive pressures make downside more likely.

Stock Performance

Over the last year, TSHA shares reached a high of over $4.00 in late June 2024. Since then, shares have trended down. On April 11, 2025, the stock closed at $1.245, with 867,477 shares traded. Based on data from Seeking Alpha, the company has a market cap of $255.23 million and 250.1 million shares outstanding.

Pipeline Developments

We think Taysha’s pipeline today really boils down to one late‑stage asset, TSHA-102, after rights to earlier programs were returned to partners.

TSHA-102

TSHA-102 is a self‑complementary AAV9 gene therapy designed to deliver a regulated MECP2 transgene via a microRNA‑responsive auto‑regulatory element for the treatment of Rett syndrome. FDA granted Fast Track Designation to TSHA-102. TSHA-102 is currently in clinic. The Phase 1/2 REVEAL trial is split into Part A and Part B. In Part A, ten patients—six at a higher dose and four at a lower dose—were dosed by February 2025, with no treatment‑related serious adverse events or dose‑limiting toxicities reported.

Early data showed improvements in breathing, sleep, social interaction, motor skills, and seizure control in a handful of low‑dose subjects out to 52 weeks. For example, one adult patient sat unassisted for the first time in over a decade, and two pediatric patients reached new developmental milestones, which were all without safety concerns.

However, in our view, the dataset was small and uncontrolled, which makes it difficult to analyze the clinical viability of the asset. Six additional patients have now been dosed at higher exposure levels, but the company has not shared any efficacy data for those cohorts. In the latest earnings call, the company indicated data will be released in the first half of 2025, which we (as well as the company) see as a major catalyst for the stock.

We think regulators will want to see how the therapy fares in a significantly larger cohort that is placebo-controlled. Without a sufficient population size and adequate controls, we think efficacy will be difficult to demonstrate to regulators. We are therefore bearish on the continuing development of TSHA-102.

It appears that Taysha still maintains an option agreement with Astellas Pharma (OTCPK:ALPMY). Under that deal, Astellas invested $50 million for a 15% equity stake, and it holds an exclusive option to license TSHA-102. While that provides non‑dilutive funding and potential help with commercialization efforts, it is worth noting that the option has not yet been exercised (and may never be).

This is particularly concerning to us as Astellas previously walked away from TSHA 120 in giant axonal neuropathy due to what appears to have been a disagreement between the two companies over the avenue to pursue further clinical development of the asset based on the FDA's feedback regarding trial parameters.

At this point, we believe Astellas is waiting to receive the full safety and efficacy readout data for TSHA-102 before making a decision on whether to exercise its option. We see this as permissible under the terms of the option agreement, notwithstanding the redactions contained within the document.

Aside from Taysha's option agreement with Astellas, according to an SEC filing as well as Abeona Therapeutic's (ABEO) website, Abeona – which is a company we recently featured in our article here) – has assisted Taysha by providing certain IP related to the development of TSHA-102. Through this relationship with Taysha, Abeona is entitled to an upfront license fee and other payments (which were realized in or around 2020), and, more importantly, "high single-digit" royalties for TSHA-102 and any other Rett product.

Other Programs

We think the company's other programs have been underwhelming to date. Most other programs, including giant axonal neuropathy, Leigh syndrome, adult polyglucosan body disease, and CLN1 Batten disease have been returned to partners.

That being said, Taysha does have a licensing agreement with Abeona Therapeutics for ABO-202, a CLN1 plasmid, which appears to be in Phase 1/2, but with insufficient available data to fully assess value potential, in our opinion.

Market Opportunity

It is important from an investment perspective to get a better understanding of what Taysha may stand to gain from TSHA-102 if the therapy is commercialized. Rett syndrome affects an estimated 15,000 to 20,000 patients across the US, Europe and the UK, with no approved therapies. The market is expected to be worth $419.4 million by 2034.

The company has neither guided on what peak sales might be for TSHA-102, nor potentially when peak sales may be achieved. However, from our research above, it seems reasonable to conclude that the most sales could end up being by 2034 is $419.4 million, which assumes 100% market share.

If TSHA-102 is able to get approved and commercialized and remain the only therapy on the market until 2034, we think it is feasible that it would obtain significant market share. However, a lot would have to go very well for the company to get to that point. And even then, it appears that the company would have to share some of the revenues with Abeona, based on the "high single-digit" royalties the latter is entitled to under their license agreement, along with undisclosed royalty and milestone payments it would owe to Astellas (assuming Astellas exercises its option under the Option Agreement).

On top of that, we think it is plausible that TSHA-102 may not be alone in the Rett market, even if it gets approved. After all, there are other candidates moving through the clinic for this indication. If any one of those gets approved, it could reduce market share for TSHA-102 in what appears to be an already-narrow market.

Financial Overview

As of December 31, 2024, Taysha reported $139.0 million in cash and equivalents, which the company expects will last until Q4 2026. Cash flow from operations in Q4 2024 was -$18.3 million.

Annualizing, the Q4 run rate implies roughly $73 million of cash burn per year. At that pace, we calculate the existing cash balance will provide about 1.9 years of runway, which aligns with the company’s own estimate.

In our view, however, any expansion of trial activities or new program initiations will accelerate spending, which, in our opinion, makes a 2026 capital raise likely.

Risks

So far, we have discussed what we see as the significant risks posed by going long on Taysha. Now, we will discuss the other side of the coin, which is the risks we see associated with selling shares of Taysha.

In particular, the stock price could surge on strong clinical data from TSHA-102. Moreover, the company could benefit from significant advancements in other programs, which could send shares higher. The market opportunity for TSHA-102 could be greater than we have indicated based on incorrect market research.

Lastly, our prediction that the company could return to the capital markets in 2026 may be incorrect for a multitude of unforeseen reasons. In any such case, it would protect shareholders from further dilution next year.

Conclusion

In our opinion, now is not the time to invest in Taysha, as the company faces (1) a high binary risk associated with its lead Rett syndrome gene therapy, TSHA-102, (2) a narrow market opportunity, and (3) the potential need for additional capital in 2026. For these reasons, we initiate a Sell rating, as we believe it is more likely than not that the stock will decline further.

data.title: It's Still Early For Corcept: With Multiple Growth Opportunities Ahead, It's No Longer Just A One Drug Wonder
data.description:
Corcept Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CORT) $68.62/share.

Range Last 12 Months: $20-$117

Market capitalization: $7.2 billion Beta: 0.14 Yield: 0%

Previously written Seeking Alpha articles:

  • 9/15/2024 Initiation Article: "Corcept Therapeutics - Three Dynamic Catalysts To Reaccelerate Growth ($35.57/share)
  • 10/15/2024: "Corcept: Key Announcements Coming Soon ($45.43/share)

Recent and Anticipated Announcements:

We believe recent and potential upcoming announcements are likely to elevate Corcept in the next 12 months from a single-product, single-market, and double-digit growth company facing generic competition into a faster-growing, multi-product, multi-market Platform Growth company that can deliver rapid revenue and earnings growth for many years to come.

  • (1) On March 31, 2025, Corcept announced that its Phase 3 Rosella Trial of Relacorilant in patients with platinum-resistant ovarian cancer met its primary endpoint of "improved Progression-Free Survival (PFS)... Patients treated with relacorilant in addition to nab-paclitaxel chemotherapy experienced a 30% reduction in risk of disease progression compared to patients treated with nab-paclitaxel alone." Relacorilant for platinum-resistant ovarian cancer would initiate entry into a completely new oncology market for Corcept offering a new treatment option in combination with nab-paclitaxel that in the trial improved the interim median overall survival (OS) from 11.5 months to 16.0 months of survival. This result is comparable to Elahere, a current treatment by Immunogen/AbbVie that delivered $479 million in revenues in 2024. We believe that Relacorilant could eventually command a majority of the market since: (1) Relacorilant can address 100% of the platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (PROC) market vs. Elahere which is approved for less than half the market, and (2) Relacorilant does not have the serious side effects of Elahere. Based on Immunogen's July 2023 estimate of 22,000 PROC patients in the U.S. each year and assuming a similar number in Europe, assuming typically 6 cycles of treatment/patient per year, assuming market pricing for Relacorilant similar to Elahere's $35,000 per cycle, and assuming Corcept's likely 65% gross-to-net discount, we estimate the total potential market might be about $2.7 billion per year in the U.S. and a somewhat smaller amount in Europe. We estimate about $452 million of revenues, or about 16% of the U.S. market, available for Corcept in 2028. For launch timing, Chief Development Officer Bill Guyer said in the 2024 Q4 press release that, "We look forward to presenting the Trial's full results in the coming months. We expect to submit our NDA (to the FDA) in the 3rd Quarter and our MAA (in Europe) shortly thereafter." We believe Corcept will ask for “Priority Review” in the U.S. and that there is a good chance it will be granted given the superior efficacy and safety profile of Relacorilant for PROC, which implies a U.S. launch possibly in the First Half of 2026. This could be a significant catalyst, possibly 9–15 months from today, with a European launch shortly thereafter, although we have not yet added European PROC revenues to our financial model.
  • (2) Importantly, we believe the successful ROSELLA trial results signal the size and breadth of opportunities ahead for Corcept to identify a wide variety of disease-modifying and treatment-enhancing opportunities within the under-researched and undeveloped cortisol modulating pathway. We believe Corcept will no longer be just a "one product wonder." In our view, Relacorilant is just the beginning for Corcept as it has identified over 1000 new cortisol-modulating compounds that can target a variety of diseases and Relacorilant is just the first candidate likely to produce significant revenues for its initial market for hypercortisolism and now for its second major market, platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. We expect that Corcept will have articles on Relacorilant presented in leading medical journals throughout the year, generating more publicity, interest and support for the stock as investors begin to perceive that Corcept has a wide open field of opportunities to develop new compounds for some very large markets. Our estimates of market and product expansion opportunities are described in detail in our two earlier Seeking Alpha reports on September 15, 2024: Corcept Therapeutics - Three Dynamic Catalysts To Reaccelerate Growth and on October 15, 2024: Corcept: Key Announcements Coming Soon. We expect that from its pipeline of R&D candidates, Corcept will announce addressing every year at least one or two new therapeutic end markets and add more potential new products to its multi-market Platform Growth strategy.
  • (3) On the February 26, 2025 Q4 2024 conference call, Corcept discussed development activity in early stage prostate cancer, ALS (Lou Gehrig's disease) and MASH (metabolic dysfunction associated steatohepatitis, previously called NASH). For Early-Stage Prostate Cancer, another solid tumor, Corcept's collaborators at the University of Chicago are investigating whether cortisol modulation can block the escape route of prostate cancer from chemotherapy and are enrolling patients for a placebo-controlled Phase II trial. In 2021, the CDC estimated there were 236,659 new cases of prostate cancer in the U.S. and that 70% were local (early stage). For ALS, unfortunately, Corcept's recent DAZALS Phase II trial failed to show improvement in the ALS Functional Rating Scale (muscle movement) but did show “statistically significant improvement in patient survival as no deaths occurred in the 83 patients who received Corcept's dazucorilant while 5 deaths occurred in the 82-patient placebo group”. We have learned that apparently all ALS patients have elevated cortisol, which tends to cause a variety of health issues, so it is possible that controlling cortisol could improve quality of life and possibly improve mortality outcomes. Importantly, for ALS, Corcept expects "to receive 1-year overall survival results early in the second quarter" (i.e., possibly this month). If the DAZALS trial shows favorable results, we believe Corcept could go immediately to the FDA for an accelerated review given the need for treatments for ALS, and dazucorilant could address an important 3rd therapeutic market for Corcept's family of cortisol modulating compounds. The CDC estimates that there are about 30,000 with ALS in the U.S. For MASH or "fatty liver disease," Corcept said on its February 26, 2025, Q4 conference call that it is currently enrolling patients for a Phase IIb MONARCH trial for miricorilant. Patients in Corcept's Phase Ib trial for MASH "experienced a 30% reduction in liver fat and improvements in liver enzymes, markers of fibrosis and key metabolic and lipid measures...and was also very well tolerated with none of the GI side effects which commonly arise in patients being treated for MASH." If miricorilant is proven safe and efficacious, it could address a very large market, as the American Liver Foundation estimates that "9 to 15 million adults have MASH." Depending on test results, Miricorilant could add an important 4th therapeutic market to the Corcept's cortisol-modulating Growth Platform.
  • (4) On February 15, 2024 Corcept announced Preliminary Results From Prevalence Phase Of CATALYST Clinical Trial showing that "24% (of 700 Difficult-to-Treat Type II Diabetics) were identified as having hypercortisolism." From this trial data, we estimated that the market for excess cortisol is 60 times larger than the 10,000 traditionally assumed by the medical community (see our prior 2 Seeking Alpha articles.). While Corcept has been serving the hypercortisolism market for 15 years, this extension study suggested that excess cortisol can also cause Type 2 diabetes in 24% of patients that are "Difficult-to-Treat," i.e., not responding to insulin, GLP-1 or other traditional treatments. While it will take time to educate general practitioners and diabetologists of the need to test their non-responding Type II diabetics for excess cortisol, the dexamethasone test is simple and inexpensive and should accelerate the addition of new prescribers and patients over the next few months and years ahead. Corcept's President of Endocrinology Sean Maduck summed up the opportunity ahead on the 2024 Q4 conference call: "We... are more confident than ever that we're on track to grow our hypercortisolism business to (a range of) from $3 billion to $5 billion in annual revenues in 3 to 5 years. This market is expanding and expanding rapidly." Please note that our 5-year earnings model is more conservative and assumes only $2.29 billion of Hypercortisolism revenues in 3 years (2028).
  • (5) On March 3, 2025 the FDA officially received the submission of Relacorilant, Corcept's superior new product for FDA approval for hypercortisolism with superior safety and efficacy versus Corcept's current Korlym medication, which is the only approved medicine for hypercortisolism. The FDA assigned a Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) target action date of December 30, 2025 which means the FDA could approve Relacorilant for hypercortisolism at the end of 2025 if not earlier. Approval should limit the threat from Teva's already approved generic in Corcept's traditional hypercortisolism market as well as for approximately 24% of Difficult-to-Treat Type II diabetics, and FDA approval could provide a positive catalyst by the end of this year.
  • (6) On its February 26, 2025, Q4 2024 conference call Corcept announced that its Q4 revenues and earnings fell short of expectations due to the inability of its exclusive outside third-party Specialty Pharmacy to fulfill Corcept's surge in new prescription requests for Korlym and created a correction in the stock price. However, we believe that more importantly, the surge in new prescriptions simply validated the medical community is getting the message and the TAM (Total Available Market) for treating excess cortisol is much larger than previously believed as described in our two earlier Seeking Alpha articles. While it is possible that the First Quarter of 2025 could have some residual effects from the struggles of the outside Specialty Pharmacy to adjust to higher Rx requests and the normal beginning-of-the-year insurance renewals, we would not be surprised if on the 2025 Q1 conference call at the end of this month management gives more specific positive commentary on the greater implications of its favorable ROSELLA platinum-resistant ovarian cancer trial as well as on trends in patient and prescriber activity for its traditional hypercortisolism and new Type 2 diabetic patients. Also note that on the Q4 conference call, Corcept guided to 2025 full-year revenues of $900-950 million for a 33-40% gain versus the $675 million reported for 2024. CEO Belanoff said, "we've really seen a... pickup in the second half of last year, and we think that is going to continue through this year, accelerating as the year goes along." This provides opportunities to report strong revenue growth for the last three quarters of 2025 at the ends of July and October and in early February 2026.
  • (7) On March 10, 2025 Corcept announced the initiation of the "MOMENTUM Trial to Determine Prevalence of Hypercortisolism in Patients with Resistant Hypertension." This trial is intended to investigate the potential for another large new previously undiagnosed excess cortisol market for “resistant hypertension” sufferers whose high blood pressure doesn't respond to normal antihypertensive medications because their hypertension etiology could be a direct result of excess cortisol. The "Treatment Resistant Hypertension" market was estimated in an American Heart Association study in 2018 to have been 10.3 million US adults. Although there likely will be an overlapping of both the prevalence of hypercortisolism causing hyperglycemia in Type II diabetics and the prevalence of hypercortisolism causing Treatment Resistant Hypertension, we note that Corcept last year only served a little over 2000 hypercortisolism patients, so adding only 20,000 more potential patients whose hypertension may be caused by excess cortisol could add an order of magnitude more revenues for Corcept in the years ahead. This study will be analogous to the short one-year time frame it took to prove efficacy in an “extension study” examining possible hypercortisolism prevalence in the Difficult-to-Treat Type II diabetics market and Chief Development Officer Bill Guyer said "We expect results from this study by year-end," so an announcement of favorable data on Treatment Resistant Hypertension prevalence provides another potential catalyst for Corcept in the next 9–10 months.
  • (8) On April 7, 2025 Corcept announced the initiation of "BELLA," a Phase 2 trial for Relacorilant plus nab-paclitaxel and bevacizumab in patients with platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. Corcept stated that "BELLA will examine whether combining relacorilant with two medications... will offer patients an additional treatment option." We reiterate that Corcept believes that cortisol modulation can work by making chemotherapy better at fighting cancer, as cortisol is anti-apoptotic while chemotherapy is trying to cause apoptosis or cancer cell death. We point out that for the ROSELLA trial, Corcept recruited patients who had received prior treatment of bevacizumab and 1 to 3 other prior lines of treatment and were beginning to no longer respond to chemotherapy and becoming categorized as “platinum-resistant” ovarian cancer (PROC) patients. Since these patients had already received bevacizumab and nab-paclitaxel chemo, this study could support the use of relacorilant at earlier stages in the treatment of ovarian cancer for a larger population than just the later stage recurrent chemo-resistant PROC patients. Beyond the use of relacorilant for PROC, a favorable BELLA study might open the door wider for the addition of relacorilant or other cortisol-modulating compounds to nab-paclitaxel or other chemotherapies and with bevacizumab or other monoclonal antibodies to treat other tumor types that express the glucocorticoid receptor (GR). We note that bevacizumab (Avastin) has been used with over 3.5 million patients in over 130 countries for a wide variety of cancers including colorectal, non-small cell lung, ovarian and breast cancer according to an article published by "cancer MDPI" in July 2024. We would expect Corcept to complete enrollment around the end of this year and provide additional insight for investors at that time.

Corcept Is Reducing Risk for Its Investors As It Is Diversifying Its Products and Served Markets

It is important to realize that Corcept in the next 12-18 month period ahead may be benefitting from a rare combination of accelerating its future revenue and earnings growth rates while at the same time reducing the risk of volatility and surprises by adding new products and diverse new therapeutic markets.

If Relacorilant is approved for hypercortisolism by the FDA's PDUFA date in 8 months as we believe, this will likely reduce the threat of generics for hypercortisolism well into the next decade and at the same time, with its much improved safety profile than Corcept's current Korlym medication, help accelerate the rate of growth and increase the market penetration of the larger pool of 10,000 traditionally diagnosed potential hypercortisolism patients plus the much larger recently identified pool of Difficult-to-Treat Type 2 diabetics with hypercortisolism which we estimated in our September 2024 Seeking Alpha article at 600,000 patients.

Adding new products and addressing diverse new markets will go a long way to producing a revenue stream that is more stable, durable and predictable while at the same time accelerating growth by entering large new markets.

In almost two decades, Corcept has already done much of the heavy lifting with missionary R&D investing to better understand the cortisol pathway as well as investigating along with several universities the effects and "methods of action" of different cortisol-modulating compounds on multiple unaddressed diseases, e.g., we were impressed to learn that Corcept and its academic partners have identified 3 different methods by which excess cortisol can protect cancers, support cancer tumor growth or diminish the effectiveness of anti-cancer agents. They are in the process of exploring compounds that could bring multiple discovery dividends in the years ahead by improving success rates, reducing surprises and adding more growth layers to Corcept's Platform Growth™ trajectory.

We believe Corcept's Financial Models Support Current Valuations Despite Recent Stock Appreciation as Multiple Growth Opportunities Provide Potential for Additional Upside

My partner Ben Bratt has adjusted the timetable for Relacorilant for Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer out by one quarter to Q2 2026 but slightly increased projected revenues due to the more favorable ROSELLA Phase 3 data. With a better feel for timing with the clock now started by the FDA suggesting possible approval of Relacorilant for hypercortisolism by the end of the year and the pivotal Phase 3 data now published for Relacorilant for ovarian cancer, we present our forward estimates by quarter. We note, however, that with so many opportunities for Corcept to ramp spending to add new products and new therapeutic areas to its growth platform as well as possible delays at the FDA and other federal agencies, estimating the timing of quarterly revenues and earnings can be difficult:

As a result of the Specialty Pharmacy's fulfillment problems and the very positive Relacorilant platinum resistant ovarian cancer ROSELLA Phase 3 data which increases the likelihood for higher R&D and SG&A spending for accelerating additional cancer and other therapies, we have reduced our annual EPS estimates slightly for 2025 and 2026, but importantly increased our estimate for 2027 from $6.34 to $6.91 and increased our estimate for 2028 from $8.86 to $10.10 to reflect expected gains from relacorilant for hypercortisolism and for platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. Prior estimates for 2025 and 2026 were $2.11 and $4.10.

Note, however, that we think there is a significant possibility that our new earnings estimates could be exceeded depending upon how quickly Corcept's expanding sales force can reach more doctors and whether the third party outside Specialty Pharmacy can staff up fast enough to handle the growth in new patients and new prescribing doctors.

On the Q4 2024 conference call on February 26, 2025, CEO Joe Belanoff stated that, "Each successive quarter of 2024 brought a record number of new Korlym prescribers and patients receiving Korlym. Our rapidly increasing number of physicians now know that hypercortisolism is much more prevalent than was previously assumed. They are screening and treating many more patients than ever before. We are confident that our Cushing's syndrome business will continue to grow for years." We reiterate that Korlym and Relacorilant are maintenance therapies, so each new patient will add incrementally to revenue growth.

Valuation

Using our estimates from the table above, on April 11, 2025, Corcept at $68.62/share was selling at 38.7 x $1.77 earnings we estimate for 2025, 17.5 x $3.92 for 2026, 9.9 x $6.91 for 2027 and 6.8 x $10.10 for 2028. (Caveat emptor as usual, especially given that Corcept has so many moving parts and significant events in the next 12–18 months.)

We should point out that on March 31, 2025, Corcept's stock surged 108% to $114/share from a likely squeeze on short sellers when Corcept announced its favorable Phase 3 ROSELLA trial results for Relacorilant for Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer. The stock has since settled down, however, at $68.62/share, CORT is still up 25% from the pre-ovarian cancer announcement price of $54.62/share.

At $68.62/share we believe that Corcept is attractively priced as a long-term Platform Growth Stock™ that is still very early in its evolution given the anticipated launches of Relacorilant to serve two very large and growing markets for hypercortisolism and ovarian cancer in the next 12–18 months and with multiple other new compounds being developed for other disease indications.

On April 11, 2025, the S&P 500 at 5440 was selling at 17.7 times next year's (2026) earnings estimate. Projecting ahead, if we use that same 17.7 P/E ratio to project where Corcept could be in 2027 valued on the next year's (2028) earnings, in our view in two years (2027) Corcept could conservatively command at least a multiple of 17.7 times the earnings of $10.10/share we have estimated for Corcept for 2028, for a projected value of $178/share.

Accordingly, we are raising our price target for CORT to $178/share in 2027, up from our prior 2027 price target of $133/share from before the ovarian cancer phase III trial results and the filing of Relacorilant for FDA approval for hypercortisolism. This could produce more than a doubling of Corcept's current $68.62 stock price if it is able to deliver on its multiple growth opportunities over the next two years, macro-political events permitting.

Risks

  1. With 20.0% net margins and strong free cash flow in 2024, $603 million of cash and no debt as reported in the December 31, 2024 balance sheet and press release, Corcept bought back $38 million of its stock last year, and we believe it has low financial risk.
  2. Threat that Teva makes market inroads with its approved generic form of Korlym, but we hear that it has not made much progress and we believe as we described in our prior reports that cortisol modulation requires patient consultations and monitoring, making it less likely to be easily substituted by generics.
  3. Threat of additional generic Korlym entries beyond Teva in the next year or so, but that risk diminishes each month as Relacorilant approaches its PDUFA date, especially given the safety issues of Korlym versus Relacorilant.
  4. There are two remaining lawsuits, one with Teva accusing anticompetitive conduct by Corcept to stifle generic Korlym sales and another by a group of insurers (Aetna, Human, Molina). While we believe that the pending approval of Relacorilant will neutralize the effects of generics beginning in early 2026, there could be headline risk over the next few months.
  5. The Trump Administration could make such significant cuts to the staff of the FDA that it could cause some delays in the approval of all new drug applications.
  6. Risk that a Major Pharma may try to take over Corcept, however, the recent tripling of the stock appreciation may have reduced this risk at least in the near term.
  7. There is a risk that at times in the next few years, Corcept's management and board decide to accelerate spending ahead of its growth rate of reported revenues, which might impact profit margins temporarily, but we would likely regard this risk as a “high-class problem” of timing.

I wish to thank my partner, Benjamin Bratt, for his advice and for constructing the financial models.

This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition—open to all analysts—is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Eurozone Recovery Facing Serious Headwinds
data.description:
By Peter Vanden Houte

Ever-changing moods

When it comes to the challenge of predicting the eurozone's growth outlook, Crowded House's classic "Four Seasons in One Day" comes to mind – it perfectly sums up the constant state of flux we're seeing in the economic environment at the moment.

Germany's fiscal U-turn and the promise of increased defence spending across Europe had raised hopes for accelerated growth starting next year. This optimism was reflected in improved confidence indicators, particularly in the expectations component. However, the announcement of blanket US import tariffs of 20% on the European Union, with even higher rates for specific sectors, drastically altered the mood. To be sure, on the back of the ensuing financial market turmoil, President Trump decided on a 90-day delay for the "reciprocal" tariffs, but in the meantime the "standard" tariff of 10% has been introduced for European goods and the higher tariffs for specific sectors, like cars, still stand. Things can, of course, shift quickly – but if the tariffs remain in place this year, the impact will be sizeable.

Economy coming to a standstill

The composite PMI rose for the third consecutive month in March, driven by increased sales. This may have been temporarily influenced by US companies replenishing stocks in anticipation of higher import tariffs. Consequently, growth in the first quarter was likely positive, with the eurocoin indicator – a measure of the underlying growth trend – reaching its highest level since 2022 in the first quarter.

However, recent tariff announcements have altered the landscape. We estimate the direct negative impact on exports to be between 0.1% and 0.3% of GDP, consistent with earlier estimates from the European Central Bank. The ongoing uncertainty is also expected to negatively affect business investments, and consumers are likely to become more cautious due to international tensions. There may be a small negative wealth effect on consumption (-0.1% to -0.2%) due to tanking stock markets.

Overall, we now expect the economy to stagnate in the second and third quarters of this year, resulting in 0.5% GDP growth for the year. With Germany's planned expansionary budget, improvement is still likely in 2026, but due to a weaker carry-over effect, we have also reduced next year's growth forecast to 1.1% (down from 1.4%). It is important to note that these forecasts are highly uncertain as the trade situation may swiftly change again.

The improvement in German business sentiment was mainly due to expectations

Source: LSEG Datastream

Trade war is rather deflationary

Eurozone HICP inflation fell to 2.2% in March, with core inflation at 2.4%. The decline was welcomed by financial markets, but the month-on-month figures didn't show much deceleration in services prices. The late Easter could also push services inflation up again in April.

Even so, we think the unfolding trade war is more likely to have a deflationary rather than an inflationary impact on the eurozone. Even if the European Commission retaliates with tariffs on US imports, it is important to remember that these only account for a tenth of total imports. Meanwhile, energy prices have already fallen due to expectations of a global economic slowdown. What's more, with China facing much steeper US import tariffs, it is likely to redirect some of its excess capacity to Europe, exerting downward pressure on prices. These factors, along with weaker growth in the eurozone, are likely to keep inflation in check in the second half of the year.

Forecast for terminal ECB rate lowered to 1.75% (again)

The ECB, which was less dovish during the March meeting, is likely to change its tone again in April if Trump's tariff plans remain in place. Following the announcement of Germany's fiscal plans, concerns rose of an increase in medium-term inflation, prompting us to raise the terminal ECB interest rate to 2.25%. However, the short-term recessionary risk may now necessitate a temporarily more accommodative policy, bringing us back to our call from the pre-fiscal stimulus era. We now anticipate two 25bp rate cuts in the second quarter and a final one in the third quarter.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: China Holds Strong As Trade War Crescendos
data.description:
By Lynn Song

Sharp tariff escalations quickly send Trade War 2.0 into full swing

President Trump shocked markets on 2 April with a stronger-than-anticipated “Liberation Day” tariff hike, which sparked a series of back-and-forth escalations and retaliations.

As of the time of writing, Chinese exports to the US are now subject to 145% tariffs, while US exports to China are subject to 125% tariffs. At these tariff levels, we expect the majority of trade with viable substitution products will effectively be phased out, leaving only products with no viable alternative suppliers. Further tariff escalations are more likely to hurt the importing country at this point, and China has indicated that it would be ignoring further US tariff hikes. We have seen that China has indeed shifted toward non-tariff responses in tightening export controls and reducing Hollywood imports, and these sorts of non-tariff barriers could be the focus of any further escalation.

There are fears of a hard decoupling and an end to bilateral dialogue, but both sides have signalled that they are open to talk, only on their terms and from a position of strength. Our view is that this has become a test of endurance. Whoever blinks first will likely come to the negotiating table in a weaker position. It's yet to be seen if the carveout of smartphones, computers, and semiconductor exports (which accounted for over a fifth of Chinese exports to the US in 2024) from the broad-based country tariffs will ultimately play out as "blinking first."

We expect talks to resume at the working level, though a top-level dialogue could remain elusive until a framework for a deal is broadly in place. A lack of clarity on what the Trump administration actually wants beyond narrowing the trade deficit and reshoring manufacturing acts as an impediment to negotiations.

As always, the tariff impact will be very difficult to gauge amid so many moving pieces. The faster-than-expected escalation, though, pushes the implications for growth toward the higher end of our 0.4-0.8pp range in terms of GDP impact. It’s still uncertain how tariffs will affect the substitution effect for exports or China’s own re-exports – a pause in tariffs on the rest of the world should keep re-export channels open at least through the second quarter. Early anecdotal reports indicate a sharp slowdown of orders and shipments, but official data could take another few weeks to trickle out.

Many Chinese exporters and US importers are likely in wait-and-see mode as well, given the front-loading of imports in previous months and the volatility of tariff news over the past several weeks. However, after inventories are cleaned out, a key question of who will ultimately bear the burden of the tariffs will be put to the test. As we saw in the first trade war, many Chinese exports to the US ended up quite sticky despite tariffs. There were no viable alternatives from other countries. The tariff hike to 145% will soon give us a conclusive look at just how much of trade ultimately cannot be substituted.

Policymakers signal confidence but have a huge task ahead of them

Chinese policymakers have been busy over the past month. The Two Sessions set this year’s growth target at “around 5%” again, signalling confidence in growth stability despite tough external conditions. It also raised various fiscal targets, implying stronger policy support. Soon after, they announced a special action plan to boost consumption. President Xi said the government is intent on “fully unleashing” domestic demand. The early policy focus is on the equipment renewal scheme, as well as the trade-in policy to support domestic demand.

That workload isn't going to slacken any time soon; the People's Bank of China (PBoC) signalled it would further ease policy at a suitable time.

We think that the time has come and expect an interest rate cut and or a Reserve Requirement Ratio (RRR) reduction to be unveiled in the coming weeks. These moves could be bundled together to maximise the market impact of easing. Fiscal policy may take longer to roll out given the complexities involved, but any early announcements on significant measures to shore up growth would be a welcome surprise.

We've also had hints of additional policy steps which are being held in reserve if necessary. A successful effort to revitalise domestic demand would support Chinese industry and also help fill some of the void in global trade.

As Trade War 2.0 erupts, policymakers have maintained calm for now. In the near term, maintaining market stability and supporting vulnerable exporters will likely be high on the agenda.

China’s 5% growth objective will likely hinge on domestic demand

Economy had been holding up well in 1Q but stronger headwinds are ahead

Surprisingly, a strong March brought export growth up to 5.8% year-on-year in the first quarter of the year, only down a hair from 5.9% in 2024. Assuming there is no quick reversal of tariffs, we expect the drag on exports to become more apparent in the second quarter.

We are revising downwards our 2025 GDP forecast by 0.2pp to 4.5%. We are also revising down our forecast for consumer price index (CPI) inflation to 0.0% (from 0.7%). This reflects heightened price competition and worsening overcapacity amid tariff shocks.

China’s hard economic data from the first two months of the year generally beat cautious market expectations. Manufacturing and consumption both outperformed. Amid heightened uncertainties this year, trade-in policy beneficiaries – namely automobiles, home appliances, home renovation and decoration materials, and consumer electronics – will likely outperform.

Overall, the success or failure to achieve this year’s growth objective will depend on the speed and effectiveness of China’s policy response.

Content Disclaimer
